behaviors.description: Renamed files appear in their own category and are not counted as deletion + addition
behaviors.justification: This behavior depends on correctly parsing git's rename detection output. A pure unit test with mocked git objects could pass while the real code fails to use the -M flag for rename detection, or misparses the actual git diff format. We need a real git repository with a renamed file to verify the tool actually invokes git correctly and categorizes the output properly.
behaviors.test_description: Create a git repository, add a file (e.g., test.txt), commit it, rename the file to test_renamed.txt, commit again, then run the tool and verify: (1) exactly one rename is counted in the output, (2) the file does not appear in the 'deleted' or 'added' categories, (3) the renamed file is correctly identified by name.

behaviors.description: Binary files in diffs do not cause tool to crash; binary files are counted in the output
behaviors.justification: Binary files produce a different diff output format from text files (e.g., 'Binary files ... differ'). Mocking this format is error-prone and could hide parsing bugs. The tool might crash or produce incorrect counts when encountering real binary diffs. Only a real git repository with actual binary files (image, PDF, etc.) and real git diff output will reliably test this edge case.
behaviors.test_description: Create a git repository, add a binary file (e.g., a PNG image or PDF), commit it, then add another text file, commit. Run the tool and verify: (1) no crash occurs, (2) the binary file appears in the count output, (3) counts are accurate for both binary and text files.

behaviors.description: Repository with no commits produces empty report instead of error
behaviors.justification: GitPython's behavior on a repository with no commits may differ subtly from what we assume. The code might crash with an IndexError, AttributeError, or other exception when accessing repo.head, repo.commits, or similar properties. A unit test with mocks can't reliably replicate all of GitPython's edge-case behavior. We need a real git repository initialized with `git init` (no commits) to verify the tool gracefully handles this state.
behaviors.test_description: Initialize a fresh git repository with `git init` (zero commits), run the tool on it, and verify: (1) the tool exits successfully without raising an exception, (2) the output is a valid empty report (e.g., no added/deleted/modified/renamed files), (3) the output structure is intact (not malformed or missing keys).

behaviors.description: Report counts match actual repository change history
behaviors.justification: This is a core correctness invariant. A unit test with mocked git objects could verify that the categorization logic is applied correctly, but would not verify that the counts are *actually correct* against real git history. Off-by-one errors, incorrect categorization, or missed changes would only appear when testing against a real repository with a known sequence of commits. This requires Integration to build and verify a repository with expected counts.
behaviors.test_description: Create a git repository and perform a known sequence of operations: add 3 files (3 additions), delete 1 file (1 deletion), modify 2 files (2 modifications), rename 1 file (1 rename). Run the tool and verify the output counts exactly match: 3 added, 1 deleted, 2 modified, 1 renamed.

behaviors.description: Output is identical for staged and unstaged changes
behaviors.justification: This tests whether the tool reads the same changes regardless of git's staging state. A unit test mocking both staged and unstaged paths could appear to pass while the real code has a logic bug that reads only staged or only unstaged changes. The tool's output depends on which git diff commands it invokes (git diff vs git diff --cached vs git diff HEAD). Only a real repository where we stage and unstage changes can verify the tool produces identical output in both cases.
behaviors.test_description: Create a git repository with an initial commit, then modify a file without staging it. Run the tool and record the output. Stage the modified file with `git add`, run the tool again, and verify the output is identical. Unstage it with `git reset`, run again, and verify output remains identical across all three states.
